DataFrame constructed from Index or Series mutates the original object # BUG DataFrame constructed from Index or Series mutates the original object ## Description When constructing a DataFrame from a pandas Index or Series containing string data any modifications made to the new DataFrame unexpectedly mutate the original Index or Series . This occurs even when .copy is explicitly called before passing the object to the constructor the underlying array is reused rather than being independently tracked. This bug does not affect integer-backed Index or Series objects only string and other extension array types. Additionally this can break internal invariants displaying a DataFrame whose columns were modified this way has been observed to crash the IPython kernel. ## Reproducible Example ## Expected Behavior ## Actual Behavior ## Installed Versions details Run pd.show_versions for full details. details

BUG DataFrame constructor from Index Series doesn t track CoW refs properly ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description If you build a DataFrame from an Index or Series backed by an Extension Array CoW reference tracking is missing. The result is that mutating the new DataFrame can silently change the original Index which obviously shouldn t happen. On top of that we ran into IPython kernel crashes when trying to display a DataFrame whose columns had been modified this way which suggests some internal block manager invariants are getting broken too. ### Expected Behavior df.columns shouldn t be affected at all. CoW means either copy the data upfront or track the reference and copy on first write neither is happening here.

BUG Plotting with timezone-aware and normalized DatetimeIndex drops tz-info ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description While writing tests for the plotting of ExtensionArrays #64535 I noticed that under certain circumstances a DatetimeIndex used for the x-axis is converted to Period and thereby is losing the timezone information. This seems to be only happing if the DatetimeIndex is normalized i.e. a frequency freq can be determinated and if the DatetimeIndex aligns perfectly with Period e.g. time is midnight 00 00 00 for day frequency or time is on the full hour xx 00 00 for hourly. As a result of the loss of timezone information different Series with same native time but different timezone e.g. the Series above with EST respectively UTC timezone are plotted at the same times top plot whereas in reality the datapoints should be separated by 6 hours. On the contrary Series with non-normalized DatetimeIndex or a not-aligned one are correctly plotted bottom plot img width 50 height 1000 alt Image src details summary Full debug printing output of the example above summary details As it can be seen in the example graphic adding the additional datapoint to the Series for the bottom plot changes also the positions of all other data points as the DatetimeIndex is not anymore normalized. The LineData printed during the execution also confirms the transformation of the Series to list of Period objects for the first plot while in the second plot they are transformed to a list of timezone aware Timestamp objects. The issue seems to come from the use_dynamic_x function which determinates if DatetimeIndex can be converted to Period but apparently failing to do so. There also was a PR recently #63013 dealing with timezone-aware data and this function but it has gone stale since and I do not think it would solve the issue here as the path with index 1 .is_normalized is not changed. I think there are also some similarities to #15754 but there were only the tick locations affected and not the data. I also do not see a common code path anymore but I did not dig into the issue there and if it still persists. jbrockmendel You recently changed the comments in use_dynamic_x in 95405ddd7d7fefb0a05f6f271529009c201cd463 and reviewed the other PR do you have an opinion how this should be handled jorisvandenbossche You commented on the other issue maybe you also have an opinion on the topic on the hand that one is already 7 years old so no issue if not. While I was writing the example I also noticed the following but not sure how they relate 1. When I use hourly data like the following two indices then I get super weird date labels instead of something around 2000-01-01 I got 2689-12-25 and the following days. 2. Forcing alignment between both plots with sharex True makes the added datapoint in the bottom plot to jump from 2000-01-02 12 00 00 to 2000-01-02 00 00 00 my first guess is the data is converted by the PeriodConverter set for the x-axis to the first axes top plot and as the frequency is day only mightnight values are valid. ### Expected Behavior The location of data points on the x-axis should be independent from the DatetimeIndex being normalized or not. It also leads very quickly to plainly wrong plots if different time zones are involved. With the data in the example I would always expect to get the horizontal displacement between the two Series of different time zone in a common plot like it is shown in the bottom plot. I am not sure if it makes sense to keep the whole use_dynamic_x function or if it can be reformed somehow. The biggest issue seems to be the first Series is normalized so it registers a PeriodConverter to that axis but then the second Series might be a non normalized DatetimeIndex or has a different time zone but is forced into the Periods defined by the first Series. ### Installed Versions details INSTALLED VERSIONS ------------------ commit 0cdc017c38ba23a6f1759ecaf6d4bf4835fad086 python 3.11.15 python-bits 64 OS Linux OS-release 5.15.153.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2 Version #1 SMP Fri Mar 29 23 14 13 UTC 2024 machine x86_64 processor x86_64 byteorder little LC_ALL None LANG C.UTF-8 LOCALE en_US.UTF-8 pandas 3.1.0.dev0+641.g0cdc017c38 numpy 2.4.3 dateutil 2.9.0.post0 pip 26.0.1 Cython 3.2.4 sphinx 9.0.4 IPython 9.10.1 adbc-driver-postgresql None adbc-driver-sqlite None bs4 4.14.3 bottleneck 1.6.0 fastparquet 2025.12.0 fsspec 2026.2.0 html5lib 1.1 hypothesis 6.151.11 gcsfs 2026.2.0 jinja2 3.1.6 lxml.etree 6.0.2 matplotlib 3.10.8 numba 0.65.0 numexpr 2.14.1 odfpy None openpyxl 3.1.5 psycopg2 2.9.11 pymysql 1.4.6 pyarrow 23.0.1 pyiceberg 0.11.1 pyreadstat 1.3.3 pytest 9.0.3 python-calamine None pytz 2026.1.post1 pyxlsb 1.0.10 s3fs 2026.2.0 scipy 1.17.1 sqlalchemy 2.0.49 tables 3.11.1 tabulate 0.10.0 xarray 2026.2.0 xlrd 2.0.2 xlsxwriter 3.2.9 zstandard 0.25.0 qtpy None pyqt5 None details

BUG NULL pointer dereference in total_seconds ujson encoder ## Bug Description In pandas _libs src vendored ujson python objToJSON.c the total_seconds function calls PyObject_CallMethod without NULL check then uses the unsafe PyFloat_AS_DOUBLE macro on the result. ## Location pandas _libs src vendored ujson python objToJSON.c 305-309 ## Code ## Impact PyObject_CallMethod can return NULL on error. PyFloat_AS_DOUBLE is an unsafe macro that directly accesses struct fields without any NULL or type check. When value is NULL - PyFloat_AS_DOUBLE NULL dereferences NULL immediate segfault - Py_DECREF NULL also crashes This is triggered during JSON serialization of timedelta objects when total_seconds raises an exception. ## Suggested Fix ## Environment - pandas version current main - Affected API ujson.dumps pd.io.json on timedelta objects

BUG NULL pointer dereference in get_long_attr ujson encoder ## Bug Description In pandas _libs src vendored ujson python objToJSON.c the get_long_attr function calls PyObject_GetAttrString without checking for NULL before using the result. ## Location pandas _libs src vendored ujson python objToJSON.c 269-273 ## Code ## Impact PyObject_GetAttrString returns NULL if the attribute doesn t exist or raises an exception. When value is NULL - PyLong_Check value dereferences NULL reads ob_type crash - Py_DECREF value dereferences NULL crash This function is called during JSON serialization of datetime-like objects. Any object missing the expected attribute will crash the interpreter. ## Suggested Fix ## Environment - pandas version current main - Affected API ujson.dumps pd.io.json on datetime objects

BUG Use-after-free in int64ToIso result freed but still dereferenced ## Bug Description In pandas _libs src datetime date_conversions.c when make_iso_8601_datetime fails result is freed via PyObject_Free but the function does NOT return. Execution falls through to strlen result and return result using a freed pointer. ## Location pandas _libs src datetime date_conversions.c 59-69 ## Code ## Impact - Use-after-free strlen reads freed memory - The freed pointer is returned to the caller which will read use corrupted data - Can cause crashes incorrect datetime string output or memory corruption ## Suggested Fix Add return NULL after PyObject_Free result ## Environment - pandas version current main - Affected API datetime-to-ISO-string conversion paths

BUG Inconsistent nan to None behaviour ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description Context Originally reported in In our code we would regularly initiating an object of some class hydrolib-core BranchModel with some parameters set to None . These are then considered empty notset consistent with many other class method argument defaults. This is why we need to have None values in our dataframes with string dtype columns and not np.nan values . Pandas 2 supports both these values pandas 3 implemented stringdtype to be more robust. This has a na_value argument property which is set to np.nan per default. This na_value cannot be set to None since it is considered an empty argument which sort of makes sense to me . However we noticed that in pandas 3 it is actually possible to convert np.nan to None by adding square brackets - branches.replace np.nan None does not replace the np.nan values - branches.replace np.nan None replaces the np.nan values with None This is quite nice for our usecase since we can use this as a workaround. However this seems inconsistent and might be considered as a bug. If this is intended behaviour that is also fine since we can then trust that the behaviour will not change in the future. ### Expected Behavior One of these two - consistent behaviour between the two methods - confirmation that this is intended and will not change in the forseeable future. Or any other suggestion to avoid the need for a workaround. ### Installed Versions details INSTALLED VERSIONS ------------------ commit 72f2fea91530b5abb3cf2100cb22d84e504695c0 python 3.14.4 python-bits 64 OS Windows OS-release 11 Version 10.0.26200 machine AMD64 processor Intel64 Family 6 Model 154 Stepping 4 GenuineIntel byteorder little LC_ALL None LANG None LOCALE Dutch_Netherlands.1252 pandas 3.0.3 numpy 2.4.6 dateutil 2.9.0.post0 pip None Cython None sphinx 9.1.0 IPython 9.13.0 adbc-driver-postgresql None adbc-driver-sqlite None bs4 4.14.3 bottleneck 1.6.0 fastparquet None fsspec 2026.4.0 html5lib None hypothesis None gcsfs None jinja2 3.1.6 lxml.etree 6.1.1 matplotlib 3.10.9 numba 0.65.1 numexpr None odfpy None openpyxl None psycopg2 None pymysql None pyarrow 24.0.0 pyiceberg None pyreadstat None pytest 9.0.3 python-calamine None pytz 2026.2 pyxlsb None s3fs None scipy 1.17.1 sqlalchemy None tables None tabulate None xarray 2026.4.0 xlrd None xlsxwriter None zstandard None qtpy None pyqt5 None details

Potential resource leak when exception occurs during file operation ## Bug Report ### Description When an exception is raised during a file operation the file handle may not be properly closed leading to a resource leak. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open a file without using a context manager 2. Raise an exception before closing the file 3. The file handle remains open ### Expected Behavior File handles should always be closed even when exceptions occur. ### Suggested Fix Use a with statement context manager to ensure the file is always closed ### Environment - Python 3.x - Any OS This is a common Python pitfall that can cause issues in long-running applications.

TEST Plotting tests fail with matplotlib 3.11.0 color cycle returns RGB tuples instead of hex strings ## BUG Plotting tests fail with matplotlib 3.11.0 color cycle returns RGB tuples instead of hex strings ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description With matplotlib 3.11.0 released 2026-06-12 the default property cycle now returns colors as RGB tuples rather than hex strings. This causes 5 pandas plotting tests to fail - tests plotting test_boxplot_method.py TestDataFramePlots test_colors_in_theme default-expected1 - tests plotting test_series.py TestSeriesPlots test_pie_series_autopct_and_fontsize - tests plotting test_style.py TestGetStandardColors test_default_colors_named_undefined_prop_cycle 1-expected_name0 - tests plotting test_style.py TestGetStandardColors test_default_colors_named_undefined_prop_cycle 3-expected_name1 - tests plotting test_style.py TestGetStandardColors test_default_colors_named_undefined_prop_cycle 12-expected_name2 The upstream matplotlib change is Separate property cycle handling from _process_plot_var_args merged in commit ec65514 and released as part of matplotlib 3.11.0 This refactored the internal property cycle machinery into a dedicated class. As a side effect the default axes.prop_cycle rcParam now stores colors as R G B float tuples rather than hex strings. The relevant matplotlib milestone All affected APIs are public - mpl.rcParams axes.prop_cycle iterating over the cycler now yields color R G B dicts instead of color #rrggbb dicts - Line2D.get_color returns whatever format was stored in the cycle now a tuple instead of a string The behavior change is observable through these public APIs though matplotlib has never formally guaranteed a specific color format both hex strings and RGB tuples are valid matplotlib color specifications . The pandas tests assert a specific format hex strings rather than comparing semantically. ### Expected Behavior Tests should pass with matplotlib 3.11.0. The test assertions should either 1. Normalize colors before comparison e.g. using matplotlib.colors.to_hex or matplotlib.colors.same_color or 2. Compare in a format-agnostic way that handles both hex strings and RGB tuples. Additionally if get_standard_colors has a documented contract about its return format it may be worth normalizing the output within that function itself so downstream consumers aren t affected by matplotlib s internal representation changes. ### Installed Versions details summary Versions summary - Python 3.14 - pandas 3.0.3 - matplotlib 3.11.0 - numpy 2.4.6 details

ENH Support N directive in strptime parsing to_datetime Timestamp with format ### Feature Type - x Adding new functionality to pandas ### Problem Description GH-29461 PR GH-64899 added a N directive to Timestamp.strftime and the array-level strftime formatting nanoseconds as a 9-digit zero-padded number. The parsing side does not accept it so N output doesn t round-trip with the same format string Note that pandas strptime already parses nanoseconds via f it accepts 1-9 digits an extension over the stdlib s 6 so this is about symmetry and round-tripping rather than new capability. Suggested in ### Feature Description Accept N in format for to_datetime Timestamp and related parsing paths matching exactly 9 digits vs f s 1-9 . BUG to_parquet pyarrow engine opens the local file path twice ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description For a local filesystem path to_parquet with the pyarrow engine resolves the path through pandas.io.common.get_handle inside _get_path_or_handle then unwraps the handle s .name back to a string and hands that string to pyarrow. pyarrow then opens the same path a second time through its own memory-mapped multithreaded C++ I O layer which is where the read write actually happens. So every local-path call opens the destination source twice. Consequences - On POSIX a wasted open close syscall pair on every call minor but pointless . - On filesystems that finalize a file s contents when the descriptor is closed e.g. certain write-once object-store backed filesystems the empty pandas-side descriptor is closed after pyarrow has written and closed its own so the empty one wins and the file is silently truncated to 0 bytes - data loss with no error raised. ### Expected Behavior A local path should be opened exactly once. pandas should hand the string path directly to pyarrow which opens it itself without first opening it via get_handle . The repro above should print 0 Python-level opens of the destination and strace should show a single openat for the path. Non-fsspec URLs such as http s still need get_handle since pyarrow can t fetch those - only genuine local paths should skip it. ### Installed Versions details INSTALLED VERSIONS ------------------ commit 72f2fea91530b5abb3cf2100cb22d84e504695c0 python 3.11.2 python-bits 64 OS Linux OS-release 6.1.0-48-amd64 Version #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.1.172-1 2026-05-15 machine x86_64 processor byteorder little LC_ALL None LANG en_GB.UTF-8 LOCALE en_GB.UTF-8 pandas 3.0.3 numpy 2.4.6 dateutil 2.9.0.post0 pip None Cython None sphinx None IPython None adbc-driver-postgresql None adbc-driver-sqlite None bs4 None bottleneck None fastparquet None fsspec None html5lib None hypothesis None gcsfs None jinja2 None lxml.etree None matplotlib None numba None numexpr None odfpy None openpyxl None psycopg2 None pymysql None pyarrow None pyiceberg None pyreadstat None pytest None python-calamine None pytz None pyxlsb None s3fs None scipy None sqlalchemy None tables None tabulate None xarray None xlrd None xlsxwriter None zstandard None qtpy None pyqt5 None details

API how to handle time zone DST transitions in the distant future Historically by using pytz by default for time zones tz-aware data had wrong offsets for DST periods after 2038 e.g. see and and documented at scroll down to the 5th warning box sidenote we should update this . The same happens if you were using dateutil.tz time zones and the short summary is that pytz and dateutil are only parsing the Version 1 TZif files those don t include a rule for future times and therefore typically include transitions times extrapolated to the future but because of 32bit are limited to dates up to 2038. This issue was fixed by switching to zoneinfo by default in pandas 3.0 since zoneinfo supports the newer versions of TZif files and can use a rule-based logic for determining the offset for distant dates. However then after improving the performance for zoneinfo timezones to also use our internal fast binary search of the transition points in pandas 3.0.3 this changed this behaviour again because now we have to pre-populate our array of transition dates to use for the binary search and the linked PR decided to do that up to 2100 meaning we only get correct offsets up to 2100 and not for later dates see the example below . In I am extending that cut-off from 2100 to 2262. While having tz-aware data so far in the future is a bit a debatable use case having an arbitrary cut off after which it behaves differently is not ideal. Options - Decide we are OK with an arbitrary cut-off if it is far enough in the future e.g. 2262 and document this - Populate the transition data dynamically based on the range of the input data eg by default fill up to year 2100 but if the input data max year is 2150 we recalculate it up to that date and update the cached data - Use our fast-path for a normal range eg up to 2100 to cover most use cases and then for dates after that use the zoneinfo python API directly as a slower fallback -- Code illustration With pytz default in pandas 3 With dateutil With zoneinfo in pandas 2.3 or 3.0.0 - 3.0.2 With default zoneinfo in pandas 3.0.3

ENH API Architectural Audit - Are other inplace True methods bypassing CoW reference tracking ### Feature Type - x Adding new functionality to pandas - Changing existing functionality in pandas - Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description ### Background While investigating a memory corruption issue yesterday reported in #65664 and patched in PR #65675 I found a structural vulnerability in how the computation engine interacts with Pandas 3.0 s Copy-on-Write CoW architecture. Specifically df.eval new old inplace True was bypassing CoW protections. The eval engine reached into the BlockManager extracted the raw data arrays evaluated them and handed them back. Because the data took a detour through the external computation engine the CoW reference tracking metadata was stripped. The DataFrame assumed the returned array was unshared memory leading to silent memory corruption when the alias was later modified. ### The Architectural Question My patch forces an explicit .copy before assignment if inplace True inside pandas.core.computation.eval . However this raises a much larger architectural question Are there other C-engine Cython or NumExpr-routed methods that are secretly leaking CoW tracking data when inplace True is invoked ### Proposed Audit Targets We know that standard __setitem__ handles CoW perfectly. But any method that extracts raw arrays computes externally and attempts to write back inplace is at risk. I propose a systematic audit of the following inplace True methods to ensure they aren t making the same tracking error 1. DataFrame.query inplace True Since this relies on the exact same underlying pandas.core.computation engines numexpr python as eval it is the most likely candidate for a parallel vulnerability. 2. DataFrame.where DataFrame.mask When backed by external engines or fast-path Cython blocks. 3. DataFrame.fillna DataFrame.replace Specifically when operating on mixed dtypes that might force a block consolidation or split during the inplace write. 4. DataFrame.clip ### Action Items Rather than playing whack-a-mole with memory bugs I suggest we 1. Identify all inplace True paths that route through numexpr or bypass the standard NDFrame.__setitem__ API. 2. Implement a standardized CoW alias corruption test suite specifically targeting these external engine routes. 3. Determine if we need a centralized validation wrapper that explicitly checks reference counts when C-engines return data for inplace assignment. I am happy to lead the audit on .query and .where to start but wanted to bring this to the core team s attention to see if there is already a broader strategy for validating inplace safety under CoW. Thoughts ### Feature Description I propose implementing a two-phase solution to secure the CoW perimeter 1. A Centralized Testing Utility Introduce a new test utility in pandas tests copy_view specifically designed to test external-engine mutations. Pseudocode ......... 2. A C-Engine Return Wrapper If the audit finds multiple leaks in .query .where etc. rather than patching them individually we should implement a validation layer inside BlockManager.setitem_inplace or the equivalent API that explicitly asserts if pd.options.mode.copy_on_write assert result.refs is safe before mapping the C-engine memory back to the dataframe. ### Alternative Solutions Alternative 1 The Whack-a-Mole Approach Status Quo We do nothing structurally and wait for users to report data corruption bugs one by one as they upgrade to Pandas 3.0. This risks user trust in the new CoW architecture. Alternative 2 Do nothing and wait for PDEP-8 Since inplace True is broadly slated for deprecation and removal in Pandas 4.0 PDEP-8 we could argue that investing in inplace infrastructure isn t worth the time. However because users will be relying on Pandas 3.x during the CoW transition period allowing silent data corruption to exist during this window is highly dangerous. We need to secure the perimeter until inplace is officially removed. ### Additional Context This architectural concern was triggered by my investigation into Issue #65664 where I successfully patched a live CoW tracking bypass in DataFrame.eval inplace True via PR #65675.

BUG Conversion from np.ma.masked_array to pd.array does not preserve missing values ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description NumPy masked arrays are used to define arrays with missing values similar to pandas extension arrays. However converting to pd.array currently ignores the mask completely and masked values appear as regular values. Converting to pd.Series does respect the missing values but the dtype is converted to float and the original dtype is not preserved in all cases. This behavior is inconsistent with user expectations and with how pd.Series handles masked arrays. The same issue occurs with string masked arrays where the mask is also ignored by pd.array . ### Expected Behavior Masked values should be converted to pd.NA and the appropriate nullable dtype should be preserved such as Int64 or string instead of ignoring the mask or coercing to float. pd.array a IntegerArray 1 NA 3 NA Length 4 dtype Int64 ### Installed Versions pandas 3.0.0 numpy 2.3.5 python 3.12.9

BUG pandas.DataFrame.max fails on datetime64 ns ... columns with different time zones and NaT in the data ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description In dataframes with datetime64 columns max fails with a TypeError not supported between instances of Timestamp and float when 2 different timezones and NaT values are both present. As long as we have 1 timezone or no NaTs everything works as expected. The default behavior of max is skipping nans and NaT is interpreted as such correctly with the same timezone. DOC Missing docstring for enable_data_resource_formatter in printing module ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on main here ### Location of the documentation pandas io formats printing.py ### Documentation problem The function enable_data_resource_formatter in pandas io formats printing.py does not have a docstring describing its purpose or behavior. This function performs non-trivial logic including - Detecting whether the code is running in an IPython environment - Dynamically registering a formatter for the application vnd.dataresource+json mimetype - Enabling or disabling that formatter based on the enable parameter Without documentation it is unclear - What this formatter is used for - When this function should be called This makes the function difficult to understand and maintain for contributors. ### Suggested fix for documentation The current implementation currently has no docstring so developers must read and interpret the source code to understand its purpose and behavior. Adding a docstring would improve the documentation by - Clearly explaining the purpose of the function - Describing the meaning of the enable parameter This makes the function easier to understand reduces the likelihood of misuse and improves maintainability for contributors.

API tracker for single-element listlike RHS inconsistency in setitem obj key 42 or np.array 42 does different things depending on the accessor the key shape the dtype and the block structure of unrelated columns Six open issues describe pieces of this directly. Filing as a single umbrella so they can be considered together. ## Bull s-eye open issues #16864 2017 One item lists are assigned into dataframes as single items. df.loc 0 1 new_col one_item one_item stores one_item the inner string in each row instead of one_item the list . The reporter wanted to populate a list-valued column row-by-row and was surprised that single-element nested lists got unwrapped while multi-element ones didn t. #25806 2019 Effect of assigning list to DataFrame cell depends on unrelated column s type. df.loc a foo 123 stores 123 on a fresh DataFrame but the same assignment on the same DataFrame stores 123 once an unrelated column has been cast to float. Behavior depends on the block layout not on user intent. Same example shows .at and .loc diverging on the same input. #30649 2019 df.at ... some list doesn t set object dtype on column creation. Setting a list via .at to a non-existent column creates the column with the wrong dtype the first row stores the unwrapped scalar a while subsequent rows after the column exists correctly store a . Same accessor same RHS two different outcomes depending on whether the column already exists. #35006 2020 Different behavior when setting a list scalar to entire column vs filtered rows. df new list_of_arrays produces one outcome df.loc mask new list_of_arrays produces another even when the mask matches every row. Multi-step repro showing four expected cases and two unexpected ones with the same RHS construction. #53565 2023 regression from 2.0 Reassigning a Series index with a 1-D length-1 ndarray loses dimension. First s whatever np.array 1 stores shape 1 as expected. Second assignment of the same value collapses to shape . Reproducible 1.5 2.0 regression no movement since. #57923 2024 Inconsistent implicit conversion of 1-arrays inside DataFrames. df.at 0 0 np.arange 2 stores array 0 1 df.at 1 0 np.arange 1 stores np.int64 0 . Length-1 ndarrays get silently unwrapped to scalars where length-2+ ndarrays don t on the same accessor with the same dtype. Reporter notes pandas can be tricked into holding 1-arrays via applymap demonstrating the unwrap is at the setitem boundary and not a storage limitation. ## Closely related broader setitem consistency - #26333 2019 REGR assigning a tuple scalar-with-length to an object-dtype cell stopped working in 0.23 Must have equal len keys and value when setting with an iterable . - #46544 2022 DataFrame.loc and DataFrame.__setitem__ give different results for the same 2-D ndarray RHS. - #57962 2024 df.loc 1 2 np.zeros 3 into an object-dtype cell raises despite the dtype permitting it 1.4 2.x regression . - #61223 2025 .at setting an iterable fails when the column doesn t yet exist succeeds afterwards. - #15490 2017 .at .loc modifies the DataFrame even when the assignment raises. - #45769 2022 MultiIndex loc with an n 2 list assignment fails despite shape match. - #62369 2025 API question on what dtype setitem-with-expansion should infer. ## Umbrella meta - #55935 2023 PDEP-6 setitem casting the standing umbrella for setitem semantic decisions . - #27911 2019 API when to cast list-like to ndarray check len match the arithmetic-side analog of this question . - #60218 2024 ERR consistent error messages for unsupported setitem values. ## Adjacent same family peripheral - #42549 2021 list-like objects broadcast to each row 1.3 regression caused by is_list_like not detecting some sequences . - #46974 2022 DataFrame setitem with DataFrame RHS doesn t align column names. - #51386 2023 Series.__setitem__ with Series value alignment vs replacement question. - #53996 2023 DOC is the behavior of setitem with repeated keys defined - #57765 2024 Setting a 2-D numpy array as a column uses only the first column. - #61026 2025 Setting column with 2-D object-dtype array raises. Original-intent context for the carve-outs that any fix would need to revisit #19474 the explicit unwrap-on-listlike-LHS #37629 nested-container preservation #44103 force-split-path for scalar-row + listlike-col . ## Why one tracker These six bull s-eye issues are not separate bugs in separate corners they re the same inconsistency surfacing at six different accessor dtype shape expansion-vs-existing combinations on a code path that has accumulated independent decision points at multiple layers. Discussing them in one place makes the matrix visible and lets any fix or deprecation be evaluated against all six at once rather than one at a time.

BUG Index.get_indexer matches pd.NA against NaN for list input but not ndarray input Index.get_indexer gives different answers for pd.NA list vs np.array pd.NA dtype object on a NaN-containing index. The list path normalizes pd.NA to NaN the ndarray path doesn t. Index.get_loc scalar lookup also matches. This propagates into Index.drop which routes through com.index_labels_to_array producing an ndarray and so cannot drop a NaN-labeled entry given pd.NA . Surfaces from merge a DataFrame built with pd.NA ... has columns coerced to a NaN label so merge left_on pd.NA right_on pd.NA resolves keys correctly via _get_label_or_level_values which uses get_loc but fails on the right_drop path via _drop_labels_or_levels - Index.drop . The scalar-lookup paths get_loc list get_indexer treat pd.NA as matching NaN the array-lookup paths ndarray Index get_indexer Index.drop don t. Either both should match or neither should.

PERF skip redundant Index.equals calls in pd.concat ### Problem pd.concat compares the same logical pair of indexes twice 1. union_indexes all_indexes_same indexes api.py 337 walks the inputs pairwise first.equals idx for each other and returns indexes 0 when they all match. 2. Then _get_result reshape concat.py 652 re-runs new_labels.equals obj_labels per obj to decide whether reindexing is needed re-asking the same question. In the common case where input frames are separately-constructed with content-equal-but-identity-distinct column indexes 50 of all Index.equals calls under pd.concat are this duplicate. ### Numbers per call inputs pre-built outside the timer workload before after speedup --- --- --- --- 100 frames 50 cols single-block float 0.62 ms 0.50 ms 1.25x 1000 frames 50 cols single-block float 6.76 ms 5.70 ms 1.19x 100 frames 20 cols multi-block dict-built 0.64 ms 0.49 ms 1.31x 20 sibling-tables axis 1 1000 rows 0.19 ms 0.15 ms 1.25x 200 frames built fresh + concat read-many-files 7.96 ms 7.58 ms 1.05x After estimated by short-circuiting duplicate id self id other pairs with a per-concat memoization cache. ### Beneficiary Patterns that build frames separately then concat them pd.concat pd.read_csv f for f in files and equivalents. groupby.apply and pd.concat df.iloc a b for ... see zero Index.equals calls slices share the parent s Index identity _get_distinct_objs collapses them this change is a no-op for those. ### Proposed change Return an all_equal flag from union_indexes when it takes the all_indexes_same -True branch and thread it through _get_combined_index new_axes _get_result . When True _get_result skips the per-obj equals and uses empty indexers. Happy to put up a PR.

PERF cache BlockManager.get_dtypes result ### Problem BaseBlockManager.get_dtypes pandas core internals managers.py 339 rebuilds an object array on every call It s called from a number of hot paths df.dtypes DataFrame.fillna generic.py 6391 select_dtypes _get_numeric_data DataFrame.apply frame.py 15751 unique_dtypes in repr info generic.py 7136 and twice per iloc setitem indexing.py 944 975 . For wide fragmented managers each call is O nblocks Python iteration plus an object-array allocation. ### Numbers 1000-col DataFrame 335 blocks mixed f8 i8 object call current with cache speedup --- --- --- --- mgr.get_dtypes 47 us 0.08 us 608x df.dtypes 58 us 8.7 us 6.7x Caching saves the full cost on cache hits df.dtypes retains 8 us of unavoidable Series-construction overhead. ### Proposed change Add a _dtypes_cache attribute on the manager populated lazily in get_dtypes and invalidated alongside the existing _blknos _interleaved_dtype cache hooks managers.py 1411 1497 and other block-mutation sites . The same invalidation pattern is already in place adding one more entry is mechanical. ### Caveats - The cached array must not be mutated in place by callers. A quick audit of callers shows they only read it equality checks .take iteration no in-place writes but worth confirming during review. - SingleBlockManager already has only one block the cache is still cheap and consistent. Happy to put up a PR.

PERF Replace dateutil-based parser fallback with a regex-based parser in tslibs.parsing In non-ISO8601 cases we use dateutil s parser but this is 1 not-optimized pure-python and 2 parses some strings that we probably should raise on e.g. Jan 10am 5th 1234 The hard part here is deciding exactly what cases we want to allow and a deprecation cycle for cases that we disallow. Using a regex with a fixed set of formats would be significantly faster preliminary benchmarks are 5-15x on cases with month name sand 100x on cases with AM or PM . Running list of issues that this might address #17265

PERF skip to_datetime cache True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help #### Summary to_datetime ... cache True the default unconditionally runs the _maybe_cache machinery Series dtype object construction + should_cache which iterates up to 500 elements of the input even on inputs where _convert_listlike_datetimes is going to dispatch to a non-parsing path or a path where caching has been measured to never help. On these inputs cache True is strictly slower than cache False by margins ranging from a few percent to 520 at the worst. All five no-help conditions are identifiable in O 1 time before any sampling just by looking at arg.dtype the unit kwarg and the format kwarg. #### Affected input shapes all O 1 to detect 1. unit is not None input is dispatched to _to_datetime_with_unit which is a vectorized cast caching cannot help. 2. arg.dtype is np.dtype M8 already datetime64 _convert_listlike_datetimes short-circuits. 3. arg.dtype is DatetimeTZDtype already tz-aware short-circuits. 4. arg.dtype is ArrowDtype with type is Timestamp short-circuits. 5. format is not None and format mixed array_strptime is fast enough that pd.unique + Series.map costs as much as just re-parsing cache True is never a measurable win on this path. #### Reproducer #### Measurements On main today n 100k unless noted Input cache False cache True Ratio --- --- --- --- unit s int 10 unique 0.07 ms 0.78 ms 11.0 slower unit s int n 100k all-unique 0.07 ms 0.92 ms 13.4 slower datetime64 ns ndarray 0.03 ms 0.07 ms 2.6 slower DatetimeIndex w tz any n 0.01 ms 6.15 ms 520 slower ArrowDtype timestamp any n 0.01 ms 0.64 ms 50 slower ISO + explicit format n 10k 100 unique 0.82 ms 1.19 ms 1.45 slower ISO + explicit format n 10k 10 unique 0.80 ms 1.26 ms 1.57 slower The DatetimeTZ case is the worst should_cache does set islice arg 500 which on a tz-aware DatetimeIndex materializes 500 Timestamp Python objects 6 ms and that cost is paid regardless of n . A to_datetime round-trip on an already-converted DatetimeIndex of any size is dominated entirely by this useless sampling step. #### Where the caching gate lives pandas core tools datetimes.py - _maybe_cache is called from the dispatch in to_datetime lines 1081 1090 1104 before _convert_listlike_datetimes runs its dtype short-circuits lines 367 412 . - should_cache lines 156 213 does the iteration that costs 6 ms on tz-aware DTIs. #### Proposed fix Add an O 1 early-bail at the top of _maybe_cache unit would need to be threaded from the dispatch sites currently bound in the partial only . No behavior change every one of these inputs hits a short-circuit non-parsing path in the next call. #### Out of scope separate follow-ups - format None on ISO strings where format inference succeeds strptime path. Same loss profile as the explicit-format case but only detectable by deferring the cache decision past format inference. - should_cache s unique_share 0.7 threshold is calibrated for the slow dateutil path and is too generous for fast-parse inputs that get past the O 1 gate. #### Pandas version pandas main 501e319b0b .

DOC to_numeric Returns is missing Index and scalars may have other problems ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on main here ### Location of the documentation Returns ret Numeric if parsing succeeded. Return type depends on input. Series if Series otherwise ndarray. ### Documentation problem The documentation is missing these return types ### Index 1-d specifically Example Source ### Scalars Example Source ### Additionally Numeric if parsing succeeded implies there s a different return type if parsing failed but the function would actually raise in that case instead of returning if I understand correctly. API BUG .loc tuple ambiguity with MultiIndex when nlevels ndim I the human asked Claude to post the following ## Summary When a DataFrame has a MultiIndex with nlevels ndim the most common case a 2-level MI on a 2D DataFrame tuple keys passed to .loc are fundamentally ambiguous between two interpretations 1. MI row key df.loc a b means the row with MultiIndex key a b 2. Multi-axis indexing df.loc a b means row a column b Python produces the same tuple a b for both spellings. The current code resolves this ambiguity differently depending on context getitem vs setitem existing vs missing key scalar vs slice leading to a cluster of related bugs where the same syntax silently produces different results. ## Related issues ### Primary same root ambiguity Issue Status Problem ------- -------- --------- #14969 Open df.loc 0 0 returns a DataFrame or a Series depending on the dtype of the inner MI level identical syntax different interpretation #16396 Open df.loc 1 2 uses the MI interpretation but df.loc 1 2 and df.loc 2 switch to multi-axis column 2 incoherent #27248 Open df.loc 1 2019 3 4 fails on setitem-with-expansion because the MI key 1 2019 is misinterpreted as row 1 col 2019 #42603 Open df.loc foo 0 silently returns different things depending on whether 0 exists in MI level 1 proposes an ambiguity error #19110 Open df.loc existing_row new_col val adds a column instead of a partial row missing-label priority is inconsistent with present-label priority #17024 Open df.loc all 5 6 on a MI DataFrame flattens the MI to tupled strings ### See also Issue Status Notes ------- -------- ------- #39775 Open KeyError semantics for partially-missing MI keys in slices adjacent problem about what should happen when MI keys don t exist #16018 Closed cannot reindex from duplicate axis on MI expansion fixed in 1.3 #22247 Closed MI expansion with NaN level copies wrong values fixed ## Current behavior ### The resolution rule depends on context The current code applies different heuristics depending on the operation and key type getitem with scalar tuple _getitem_lowerdim _handle_lowerdim_multi_index_axis0 - Try MI key via obj.xs tup . If found return row. - If KeyError and ndim len tup nlevels re-raise MI interpretation . - If KeyError and len tup nlevels ndim raise IndexingError which is suppressed by the caller so the per-axis loop handles it as multi-axis. This means getitem silently switches interpretation when a MI key is missing getitem with slices _getitem_tuple_same_dim - Always multi-axis. df.loc 1 2 treats 2 as a column even though df.loc 1 2 treats it as MI level 1 #16396 . setitem with scalar tuple _get_setitem_indexer - Try MI key via ax.get_loc key . If found overwrite row. - If KeyError suppress fall through to _convert_tuple always multi-axis . This is why #27248 fails the missing MI key is reinterpreted as row new_column . ### The result depends on dtype Because the fallback to multi-axis only triggers when the MI lookup fails and whether it finds a match depends on what values are in the MI levels the same syntax gives different results depending on dtype #14969 ## Cases that already work The ambiguity only exists when nlevels ndim . These cases work correctly - Series with MI ndim 1 any tuple with len 1 exceeds ndim so _convert_tuple raises IndexingError Too many indexers and the fallthrough correctly handles MI keys. - 3+-level MI on DataFrame nlevels ndim 2 same mechanism _convert_tuple rejects the tuple and the fallthrough handles it. ## Why heuristics don t work I explored several heuristic approaches to disambiguate setitem when nlevels ndim 1. Check if last element is an existing column Fails for df.loc partial_key new_col val simultaneous row + column expansion . 2. Check type compatibility of each element with its MI level Fails when MI levels and columns share the same dtype e.g. int int MI + int columns . 3. Combined type + column-dtype check Handles many cases but is fundamentally a guess there exist DataFrames where both interpretations are type-valid. The ambiguity is inherent to the API not a deficiency in the implementation. ## The right API I think the right long-term answer is .loc should not guess . When nlevels ndim there should be one canonical interpretation and the user should use explicit syntax for the other. ### Option A MI key always wins .loc a b on a 2-level MI always means MI row key. Multi-axis indexing requires the explicit df.loc a b form or partial keys like df.loc a col when a is clearly a single level-0 value . Pros Consistent with getitem priority MI key is tried first . Consistent with Series. The multi-axis form df.loc ... is explicit and readable. Cons Behavior change for df.loc scalar col when the scalar matches level 0 and col matches a column. ### Option B Multi-axis always wins .loc a b on a 2-level MI always means row a col b . MI row key indexing requires df.loc pd.IndexSlice a b or df.loc a b . Pros No behavior change for existing multi-axis code. Consistent with how _convert_tuple already works. Cons Breaks df.loc a b for MI row access which is arguably the more natural reading. Inconsistent with Series and with the nlevels ndim case. ### Option C Raise on ambiguity When nlevels ndim and the key is a plain tuple raise an error as suggested in #42603 requiring the user to be explicit. Problem Python offers no way to distinguish df.loc a b from df.loc a b so we d have to reject all plain tuple keys when nlevels ndim which is extremely disruptive. ### My recommendation Option A The key observation is that the MI-key interpretation is already the first thing tried in both getitem and setitem the multi-axis path is always a fallback. Making the MI-key interpretation authoritative rather than a try except heuristic would make the behavior predictable and consistent across getitem setitem scalars and slices. Users who need multi-axis indexing on a 2-level MI DataFrame can write df.loc a b or use pd.IndexSlice . ## How to get there 1. Short term Document the ambiguity and the df.loc a b workaround in the MultiIndex docs. 2. Medium term Add a FutureWarning when the ambiguous case is hit when nlevels ndim MI key lookup fails and the code is about to fall back to multi-axis. The warning should suggest explicit syntax. 3. Long term Change the default to MI key interpretation when nlevels ndim removing the multi-axis fallback. This would resolve #27248 #19110 #16396 #42603 and #14969. #17024 MI flattening on scalar expansion is a separate sub-bug in the expansion machinery.

MultiIndex inconsistent mixed depth squeezing behavior across axes NA types and operations I the human asked Claude to post the following ## Summary When a DataFrame has MultiIndex columns and a top-level key maps to a single column whose remaining sub-level s are all placeholder values empty string or NA pandas has an undocumented convenience behavior that squeezes the result from a 1-column DataFrame into a Series. This behavior has several inconsistencies 1. NaN vs empty string squeezes to Series but NaN returns a 1-column DataFrame 2. Column vs row axis asymmetry The squeezing only happens for column indexing df key not row indexing df.loc key 3. How placeholders are created str.split expand True fills with NaN df newcol ... fills with so the same conceptual operation produces different indexing behavior ## Related issues - #11424 DOC document level-dropping for empty columns original report of the undocumented behavior - #17024 .loc with partial key flattens MultiIndex on index scalar labels auto-transformed to key on columns but not rows - #17029 Asymmetric behavior between index and columns when getting incomplete label the axis asymmetry - #25284 Empty string as sub-index changes the shape NaN vs inconsistency - #25154 MultiIndex slicing with NaNs unexpected results NaN handling in MultiIndex more broadly now closed ## Where the behavior lives The squeezing logic is in DataFrame._getitem_multilevel frame.py lines 4351-4362 Row indexing goes through NDFrame.xs MultiIndex._get_loc_level which has no analogous squeezing. ## Discussion what is the right API There are a few options ### Option A Remove the squeezing entirely df key on a MultiIndex always returns a DataFrame even if there s only one sub-level and it s a placeholder. This is the most predictable behavior the return type depends only on whether the key is in the top level of a MultiIndex not on what the sub-level values happen to be. Pros Simplest most predictable eliminates all the inconsistencies at once. Cons Breaking change for users who rely on the current behavior. The convenience of df name returning a Series for ragged column structures is real. ### Option B Extend squeezing to be consistent Make the squeezing work for all NA-like values NaN None NaT and on both axes. This means - key NaN and key both squeeze to Series scalar on column axis - key and key NaN both squeeze to Series on row axis - Standardize on a single placeholder value NaN when creating mixed-depth MultiIndexes Pros Makes the ragged MultiIndex use case work consistently. Cons The squeezing behavior is still magical and undocumented. Return type still depends on sub-level values not just the structure of the indexing operation. ### Option C Deprecate squeezing point users to explicit APIs Deprecate the squeeze and recommend users who want a Series from a 1-column sub-selection use df key .squeeze or df key . Eventually remove the squeezing in a future major version. Pros Gets to the clean state of Option A without a hard break. Cons Deprecation period is annoying for users who rely on the current behavior.

ENH df.duplicated default behavior should default to tagging all duplicates as True ### Feature Type - Adding new functionality to pandas - x Changing existing functionality in pandas - Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description Hi folks. I don t complain much about excellent open-source freeware and I m reasonably proficient at Pandas. I have about three years of solid use under my belt before that I had about six using Stata and am was a Stata maven. Pandas is generally much better...BUT... df.duplicated has what seems to me to be very confusing default behavior. In Stata for example one might use the equivalent duplicate-tagger function in Stata a command to locate people who are duplicates on a particular column to then hand inspect some of the rows to see if these are data entry errors or if by contrast one has perhaps missed a nuance and should expect a small number of duplicates on some col. This might sound sloppy but when the codebook docs for the DF sucks this is a good way to figure out precisely what it might mean for a row to be a duplicate on some subset. For that reason I propose that df.duplicated keep False should be the default behavior. I just spent around 30 minutes trying to figure out the problem with my code or possibly with the df etc. because I was trying to locate people who are duplicates on a col that should in theory have a few duplicates but not many and I could not actually see any duplicates after the following. Of course I realized that this is because the default behavior is to not tag the first duplicate as a duplicate in my case since the multiplicity of a duplicate is almost always two that meant that subsetting the data to only include duplicates was actually omitting those duplicates from view. I did even skim the Docs obviously inefficiently at first but the wording itself seems extremely confusing. I did not realize that keep False would solve my problems because this is not really about keep drop behavior but tagging behavior. Changing the option s actual name to tag or something similar would probably lead to less confusion make it more obvious that the default behavior is odd. On that note another reason to change this I think is that there is no particular reason to expect the first duplicate to be the one to keep at least without more context on the df certainly this is not true in general . This might make sense if it is a duplicate on all cols but if the subset is anything but the full set of cols this seems like behavior that might lead to very unexpected results. If someone does simply have outright duplicate records it doesn t really cost significant time or memory AFAICT to then go back and set keep first once it s been confirmed that one might as well keep any record. ### Feature Description I m not a backend dev. merely a fairly tech-savvy sociologist. I think the technical side of this would be very easy. ### Alternative Solutions People could read the docs very carefully before seeking other solutions. ### Additional Context _No response_

ENH pd.crosstab with normalize index and margins True drops the marginal Total column. ### Feature Type - Adding new functionality to pandas - x Changing existing functionality in pandas - Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description When creating a contingency table using pd.crosstab if a user specifies normalize index while also requesting margins True the resulting DataFrame includes the marginal row at the bottom but excludes the marginal column on the right . While the marginal column would mathematically consist of constants 1.0 its omission prevents the generation of a complete framed table for reporting and visualization purposes. It is normal in data science to display both row and column totals in a crosstab. If you select row or column as the sum it s expect to show 100 on the non-selected column this is supported by the grand total selection where only the total in the bottom right is 100 . This helps users quick understand what you are trying to convey and reinforces the choice of display The default should be to hide the result which is 1.0 ### Feature Description def _add_margins self table margins_name # CURRENT BEHAVIOR # Pandas calculates the sum of columns and rows. # If normalize index the row sum is always 1.0. # PROPOSED FIX # Explicitly check if normalize is index or columns # to prevent the Total column row from being optimized away. if self.normalize index # Force add the marginal column of 1.0s table margins_name 1.0 # Calculate the marginal row the distribution of the columns # This is the mean of the normalized rows or a separate total calculation column_totals table.iloc -1 .mean axis 0 table.loc margins_name column_totals table.at margins_name margins_name 1.0 return table ### Alternative Solutions none ### Additional Context _No response_

API Index.take discards fill_value and raises unnecessarily Index.take has two bugs when allow_fill True and fill_value is passed explicitly ### 1. fill_value is silently discarded The caller s fill_value is ignored self._na_value is always substituted ### 2. _can_hold_na gate raises on integer dtypes The caller provided a valid fill value that the dtype can hold but Index.take rejects it because _can_hold_na is False for integer dtypes. ### Consequences These force internal callers to bypass Index.take and work on the underlying arrays directly - frame.py idxmin idxmax algorithms.take index._values ... instead of index.take ... - reshape merge.py appends a sentinel value to the index with a comment We do not use allow_fill and fill_value because it throws a ValueError on integer indices - indexes base.py reset_index take-then-putmask as two operations - io formats excel.py guards allow_fill on levels._can_hold_na - indexes multi.py algos.take_nd lev._values ... instead of lev.take ... ### Proposed fix - Respect the caller s fill_value - Remove the _can_hold_na gate let the underlying array handle type promotion - Simplify the workaround sites This is backward-compatible the existing gate allow_fill True + fill_value is None numpy-style take is preserved so bare idx.take indices is unchanged. Separately there are questions about the right long-term defaults for allow_fill currently True for backward compat but arguably should be False to match ExtensionArray.take but that would require a deprecation cycle and is out of scope here.

ENH Simplify reading vertically stacked CSV tables ### Feature Type - x Adding new functionality to pandas - x Changing existing functionality in pandas - Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description The overall goal is reading vertically stacked CSV files with multiple tables. There is no difficulty in writing such a file with pandas just append or reading and splitting horizontally stacked CSV files but they can be inefficient and more complicated to write . This is not technically a CSV format according to RFC 4180 since column sizes differ but a very slight generalisation of it. It doesn t really have a name but has been re-invented several times with different ways of determining where to split the table. I think the only form that really needs supporting are known specified lines e.g. Col1 Col2 would split on blank rows but also on Col1 Col2 rows. I had considered when the number of columns changes but I think that s a strictly less useful format you can t have multiple tables with the same number of columns consecutively and it could be extended in later if wanted. ### Feature Description Possible solutions I can think of any one of the following #### A Part of TextFileReader chunking This would require the ability to reset the columns state of the reader either by the user or as part of the implementation . If that is easy to do then this might be the most straightforward way of implementing this feature. The simplest way of doing this is adding an option chunk_on None which can be set to a list of row values permitted to include the blank row . Importantly the presence of this does not just cause the chunks to be broken off but also the number header names and types of columns to be reset if not specified as a parameter as if reading a new file. But it does not reset if chunking because of chunksize so the two are compatible. If skip_blank_lines is True then blank lines can end if is in chunk_on but not begin or contribute to a chunk. I.e. consecutive skipped blank lines do not lead to 1x1 NA chunks do not lead to 1x1 NAs before EOF or before other chunk_on rows and do not lead to 1xN NA rows in previous or following chunks. If skip_blank_lines is False then a blank line is interpreted as a row and behaves the same as any other row perhaps aside from NA conversion . If skip_blank_lines is True and is not in chunk_on then it is as if reading the file without blank lines in. This should be the same as currently aside from the new parameter cases. Usage would look something like this #### B Implement a function to directly read vertically stacked CSV or an parameter read_csv is overloaded with . This is the simplest and fastest from the user s perspective but possibly the least convenient to implement and raises many questions about the interface. This could return an iterable forward compatible if changed to some object type of the distinct tables. One option that might not cost much in terms of implementation is to convert the existing read_csv implementation to work on a stack via growing array of the states it currently uses one of. Everything works mostly the same with the new additions e.g. new header rows but on the state at the top of the stack. If splitting is not permitted read_csv then nothing new is ever pushed to the stack beyond the first element . When a split is identified it simply pushes a new state to the stack and works on that. #### C Based on the skiprows nrows approach implement a way of reading this data from the file somehow so that the file can then be read again several times for each table. This is bad for performance because it multiplies IO operations. Also the interface is a bit questionable and specific e.g. if split by known headers - does it take a list of them and generate a new column indexing it . Basically a much worse version of A. #### D Improve the post-processing of the merged file This might be easier to implement than A and could be more generically useful but is going off the path a bit and has some corner cases. This would probably involve A splitting operation which would involve the processing steps 1 2 optionally 5 since you might want to keep the index in some cases One or more operations for re-interpreting the table with the first row as headers newly inferred dtypes removal of columns covering processing steps 3 4 6 ### Alternative Solutions There are several possible approaches for reading the file today but then splitting and processing is where it gets complex A read_csv with header None and names range N where N the number of columns in each table. An upper bound on N has to be known in advance. B Multiple read_csv calls with skiprows and nrows. This requires knowing the split indices in advance. C Use the standard library csv module and put the results into one or more dataframes either splitting there or later . This doesn t have many of the niceties of pandas read_csv including performance but If splitting with pandas there is probably room for improvement here as I am only a beginner with pandas 1. Use df.groupby df condition .cumsum to split on splitting rows 2. Optionally depending on the type of splitter remove splitting rows and empty group items or replace multiple empty groups with one 3. If there are header rows a Set each df s columns to the first row b Drop the header row c Unset the column name on the df 4. Remove columns that should not be present. If splitting by known column counts or names then keep only those e.g. df col1 col2 . If splitting by blank rows or knxown lines the best approach is probably to remove all-NA columns technically though it could still be a valid column that just happens to be all NA 5. Reset all indexes with drop True 6. If you know the types of the data convert them all. If you don t then you are out of luck and must leave them as strings or use a series of try-catches on each element with conversion operations to guess a type. Possibly you would have to set the NA parameters so that a present column is never treated as NaN might not work for float NaN As you can see this is quite involved and somewhat error prone. Arguably the reason for all this complexity is because it was not a very well defined data frame to begin with. It would be nice if some or all of this process could be simplified. ### Additional Context Related As mentioned in the related issue this is not a good format for long-term or professional storage. I think it is worth supporting to some degree because people have mentioned needing to read this format several times - not just the related issue but there are some examples on stackoverflow and the documentation link read_csv in action FAQ for multiple tables says If the file structure is complex you might need to pre-process the file or use additional libraries to extract the specific table. Pandas alone may not directly handle complex multi-table CSV files. It would be good if pandas just supported this use case directly or more easily. I do believe it has its own niche for short-term informal and internal data where the schema is implicit whatever data I happen to be dumping . The use case I have is testing information from a language that isn t Python. Reaching for HDF or SQL here is extremely heavy handed dependencies file dataset setup and unnecessary while CSV is a very natural and convenient human readable format requiring no dependencies. Because I can just write out the format myself without having to think about the binary layout. I have a couple of very big tables proper CSV files and then several small tables often metadata . I don t really want to have a single CSV file for each very small table which probably isn t very efficient in terms of IO operations and is more files to manage .

ENH Add DataFrame.memory_report for actionable dtype optimization suggestions img width 827 height 732 alt Image src ### Feature Type - x Adding new functionality to pandas - Changing existing functionality in pandas - Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description DataFrame.memory_usage tells you how much memory a DataFrame uses but gives no guidance on why or what to do about it. Users who want to optimize memory must - Manually inspect each column s dtype - Know the integer float downcast rules by heart - Manually check cardinality for object columns - Write their own loops to apply changes This is a very common pain point especially for data engineers and analysts working with medium-to-large datasets who hit memory pressure and have no built-in tool to diagnose it. ### Feature Description A new method DataFrame.memory_report that 1. Analyzes every column s current dtype and actual value range 2. Suggests the smallest safe dtype for each column 3. Explains why each suggestion is safe value range cardinality ratio 4. Returns a rich MemoryReport object that is both human-readable and 5. machine-readable 6. Never modifies the DataFrame purely diagnostic zero side effects def memory_report self verbose bool True category_threshold float 0.5 check_datetimes bool True - MemoryReport Analyze memory usage and suggest safe dtype optimizations. Parameters ---------- verbose bool default True If True print the full report to stdout. category_threshold float default 0.5 Suggest category dtype when unique ratio is below this value. For example 0.5 means fewer than 50 unique values suggest category. check_datetimes bool default True Attempt to detect datetime-parseable strings in object columns. Samples up to 200 rows to avoid scanning very large columns. Returns ------- MemoryReport Structured report with per-column diagnostics. Supports - report.to_dataframe machine-readable table - report.apply df apply all suggestions returns new df - report.actionable list of columns with suggestions - report.high_impact columns with 50 savings potential ### Alternative Solutions df.info memory_usage deep . This is the closest native.But no guidance on how to fix it. ### Additional Context Design Decisions Zero side effects. memory_report never modifies the DataFrame. Applying suggestions is always an explicit opt-in via report.apply df . Safe-only suggestions. Every suggestion is lossless by default Integer downcasts are validated against actual min max values Float32 suggestions are validated values are cast to float32 and checked for overflow before recommending apply wraps each conversion in a try except and warns on failure it never raises Null awareness. The report flags columns with nulls so users know before applying dtype changes that may affect null representation. Overflow guard for floats. The implementation detects when a finite float64 value would become inf after float32 cast and keeps float64. Composable return type. Returning a MemoryReport rather than just printing means the output is usable in notebooks CI pipelines and data quality checks.

BUG Regression - df key df key x silently drops assignment on MultiIndex columns with mixed-dtype level and single sub-column ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description _Disclaimer In writing this bug report I have used AI. This bug was found because the same code has a different behaviour in version 2.2.3 as in 3.0.1. I have verified the reproducible example in both latest versions 2.3.3 vs. 3.0.2. I m familiar with the copy on write changes but I m far from a pandas expert._ Top-level __setitem__ on a column MultiIndex silently discards the assignment no error no warning when all three of the following conditions hold 1. The DataFrame has a column MultiIndex. 2. The second level has object dtype due to mixed element types. In the example above M string is on level 1 alongside integers 0 1 2 which forces df.columns.levels 1 to object . 3. The top-level label being assigned to contains exactly one sub-column here prices 0 . Top-level labels with multiple sub-columns here earnings with 1 and 2 are assigned correctly. I isolated the trigger by varying level 1 level 1 multi sub-col earnings single sub-col prices ---------------------------------- ---------------------------- --------------------------- all int 0 1 2 divided divided all string M 0 1 2 divided divided mixed 0 1 2 M divided silent no-op So the bug is specifically the combination of an object-dtype level and a single-element top-level group going through the frame-assignment path. This is a regression from 2.3.3 3.0.x . The same code divides prices correctly on 2.3.3 and silently drops the write on 3.0.2. Workarounds that do work on 3.0.2 Workarounds that also silently fail or corrupt data on 3.0.2 Given this is silent data loss on a plain non-chained __setitem__ it seems clearly unintended rather than a CoW semantic change. ### Expected Behavior df prices df prices 100 should either divide the values in place raise or warn. It should not silently discard the assignment. The presence of a string label elsewhere on level 1 or the number of sub-columns under the selected top-level label should not affect whether the assignment takes effect. ### Installed Versions details My own verification Verification in AI sandbox details

BUG Regression when using .str ... accessor on ArrowExtensionArray ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description When using .convert_dtypes dtype_backend pyarrow the underlying array of string types is an ArrowExtensionArray . Previously this class inherited string methods from ArrowStringArrayMixin and BaseStringArrayMethods . However in 3.0.0 that was changed to remove BaseStringArrayMethods #62155 . Unfortunately BaseStringArrayMethods does not have full parity with ArrowStringArrayMixin . I did a quick search of the other differences and _str_getitem appears to be the only difference that causes this problem. I think ArrowStringArrayMixin or ArrowExtensionArray should implement the _str_getitem method though another solution would be to have .convert_dtypes dtype_backend pyarrow to produce an ArrayStringArray instead of an ArrowExtensionArray for string columns. I m not familiar enough with the details of these types to know why we would prefer the latter. ### Expected Behavior x.str 0 works for any string-typed x ### Installed Versions details INSTALLED VERSIONS ------------------ commit ab90747e3dae0e69b1bdbf083820b8075689b34b python 3.13.9 python-bits 64 OS Windows OS-release 11 Version machine processor byteorder little LC_ALL None LANG None LOCALE pandas 3.0.2 numpy 2.4.4 dateutil 2.9.0.post0 pip None Cython None sphinx None IPython None adbc-driver-postgresql None adbc-driver-sqlite None bs4 None bottleneck None fastparquet None fsspec None html5lib None hypothesis None gcsfs None jinja2 None lxml.etree None matplotlib None numba None numexpr None odfpy None openpyxl None psycopg2 None pymysql None pyarrow 23.0.1 pyiceberg None pyreadstat None pytest None python-calamine None pytz None pyxlsb None s3fs None scipy None sqlalchemy None tables None tabulate None xarray None xlrd None xlsxwriter None zstandard None qtpy None pyqt5 None details

BUG ADBCDatabase does not escape SQL identifiers in delete_rows read_table and to_sql ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the latest version of pandas. - x I have confirmed this bug exists on the main branch of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description ADBCDatabase used when passing an ADBC connection to pd.read_sql_table or DataFrame.to_sql interpolates the name and schema parameters directly into SQL strings without quoting or escaping. This allows SQL identifier injection when those parameters contain user-controlled or untrusted values. Three methods are affected in pandas io sql.py ADBCDatabase.delete_rows line 2445 vulnerable # BEFORE vulnerable table_name f schema . name if schema else name self.execute f DELETE FROM table_name .close ADBCDatabase.read_table line 2239 vulnerable # BEFORE vulnerable stmt f SELECT select_list FROM schema . table_name ADBCDatabase.to_sql line 2401 vulnerable # BEFORE vulnerable sql_statement f DROP TABLE table_name By contrast SQLiteDatabase.delete_rows already uses _get_valid_sqlite_name which wraps identifiers in double quotes and escapes internal quotes ADBC has no equivalent function and this pattern was not followed when ADBC support was added. ### Expected Behavior Table names and schema names should be quoted as SQL identifiers before interpolation the same way SQLiteDatabase already does with _get_valid_sqlite_name . Expected generated SQL -- name audit_log DELETE FROM audit_log -- name users DROP TABLE audit_log -- DELETE FROM users DROP TABLE audit_log -- treated as a literal identifier not executable SQL A new helper _get_valid_adbc_name using ANSI SQL double-quote escaping compatible with PostgreSQL DuckDB SQLite and other ADBC targets should be applied to all three methods. ### Installed Versions details INSTALLED VERSIONS ------------------ commit ab90747e3dae0e69b1bdbf083820b8075689b34b python 3.12.10 python-bits 64 OS Darwin OS-release 25.3.0 Version Darwin Kernel Version 25.3.0 Wed Jan 28 20 56 35 PST 2026 root xnu-12377.91.3 2 RELEASE_ARM64_T6030 machine arm64 processor arm byteorder little LC_ALL None LANG C.UTF-8 LOCALE None.UTF-8 pandas 3.0.2 numpy 2.4.4 dateutil 2.9.0.post0 pip None Cython None sphinx None IPython None adbc-driver-postgresql None adbc-driver-sqlite None bs4 None bottleneck None fastparquet None fsspec None html5lib None hypothesis None gcsfs None jinja2 None lxml.etree None matplotlib None numba None numexpr None odfpy None openpyxl None psycopg2 None pymysql None pyarrow 23.0.1 pyiceberg None pyreadstat None pytest None python-calamine None pytz None pyxlsb None s3fs None scipy None sqlalchemy None tables None tabulate None xarray None xlrd None xlsxwriter None zstandard None qtpy None pyqt5 None details Note adbc-driver-sqlite 1.10.0 and adbc-driver-postgresql 1.10.0 were used for reproduction installed separately .
