DOC Clarify rolling window behavior with negative offset strings and froward rolling windows ### Pandas version checks - x I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on main here ### Location of the documentation ### Documentation problem There is no documentation on a forward-looking rolling windows with offset strings. I often need to make a forward-looking rolling window on datetime data. To do so I ve tried different ways and the only one I found was using two .iloc -1 reverse the series order with the rolling occurring in the middle. nb Because the data has irregular DateTimeIndex not every 2 hours for example I can not use the pd.api.indexers.FixedForwardWindowIndexer This is what this shows - The behavior of negative offset strings e.g. -5h in Series.rolling and DataFrame.rolling is not documented and produces counterintuitive results. They don t inverse the rolling window the window just collaps into 1 element windows. The negative int values are actually prohibited by the function ValueError window must be an integer 0 or greater so I would expect the same to be for offset stings. Maybe a bug - Using .iloc -1 to invert the series before performing the rolling window suggested by works. But you still have to use a positive offset even if your dataset is anticrhonological order when the rolling occurs. ### Suggested fix for documentation For me adding something like the following text on the examples would do the tricks Rolling sum with forward-looking windows with 3 seconds df_time.iloc -1 .rolling 3s .sum .iloc -1 B 2013-01-01 09 00 00 1.0 2013-01-01 09 00 02 3.0 2013-01-01 09 00 03 2.0 2013-01-01 09 00 05 4.0 2013-01-01 09 00 06 4.0 Note Negative offset strings e.g. -5h do not create forward-looking windows and should be avoided. They collapse to single-element windows.

ENH BUG Utilize _values_for_json in Series.to_json ### Feature Type - x Adding new functionality to pandas - x Changing existing functionality in pandas - Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description ExtensionArrays can define a _values_for_json method if the standard numpy array with object dtype returned by __array__ is not serializable because the objects themselves are not serializable. However this method is only utilized for DataFrame.to_json trough BlockManager.column_arrays In Series.to_json no path leads to the _values_for_json method and nearly always __array__ is utilized. Therefore some ExtensionArrays are only JSON serializable if they are in a DataFrame and not a Series which in my opinion also could be considered a bug. ### Feature Description In short modify the objToJSON.c file to also call _values_for_json for pandas Series . With the help of GitHub Copilot I was able to trace the issue to the get_values function specifically the following lines - In 201 we are effectively calling Series.values and assign it to values . - If the Series and therefore values is not empty values now is a numpy array or ExtensionArray. - Next in 205 it is checked if values has a __array__ attribute and if so calls it that is also the path mentioned above that leads potentially to the numpy array of object dtype with not serializable objects in it. - At last in 210 if none of the checks above worked it is checked if values is already a np.ndarray and only that so no subclass and if not dereferences values and tries to create the array somewhere up the stack if I understand it correctly . My proposal would be to add another check after the empty check but before the __array__ check. Similar to the __array__ check it could test if values has a _values_for_json attribute and therefore is an ExtensionArray and if so calls that method to create the serializable numpy array. This check could work like the following snippet but I have not tried it out yet. It would be important to include it before the __array__ check as ExtensionArrays have that attribute too Disclaimer This snipped was developed with GitHub Copilot after chatting with it to understand how the get_values function works and asking if a check similar to __array__ would also work in the designated spot. It also suggested to not make it an else if block but an if block so that if the return value of _values_for_json itself is not already a np.ndarray but an array-like object the following checks could still convert that object using the __array__ attribute. In my opinion that is not necessary because the function signature of _values_for_json already specifies that the return value shall be a np.ndarray and the base implementation just calls np.asarray self which should also dispatch to __array__ . I therefore modified the snipped above accordingly. As there currently do not seem to be any test for _values_for_json adding tests that make sure that this method actually returns an np.ndarray and that the values inside are serializable seems sensible. ### Alternative Solutions Always convert your Series to DataFrame with sr.to_frame before JSON serialization then the _values_for_json method of the ExtensionArray is already used. ### Additional Context I found this issue during my work for an external ExtensionArray to support astropy Quantity objects which are subclasses of np.ndarray but themselves not JSON serializable. I also looked a bit around through the open issues regarding Series and JSON serialization and found #55426 by jbrockmendel there he wrote down some general notes about formatting of ExtensionArrays the proposed enhancement is especially connected to point 6 of the list.
